title: Best practices for free-living accelerometer data collection
summary: A practical checklist for placement, wear protocols and metadata capture in field studies.
howToUse: Adopt the checklist when writing your data-collection protocol.
why: Reduces avoidable data loss and harmonises studies across the network.
example: Used to standardise wear protocols across three cohort sites.
limitations: Focused on wrist and thigh placement; hip guidance is lighter.

title: Detecting non-wear time: a comparison of algorithms
summary: Plain-language comparison of common non-wear rules and when each fails.
howToUse: Match the recommended rule to your device and sampling setup.
why: Non-wear handling materially changes daily activity totals.
example: Guided algorithm choice for a 2,000-participant sleep study.

title: Calibration of triaxial accelerometers using local gravity
summary: How autocalibration corrects sensor bias using still periods and local gravity.
howToUse: Run autocalibration before deriving acceleration metrics.
why: Uncalibrated sensors introduce systematic error across devices.
example: Applied to harmonise two accelerometer brands in one cohort.
limitations: Needs sufficient still periods within each recording.

title: From raw acceleration to physical activity intensity: a methods primer
summary: Accessible walkthrough of how raw signals become minutes of activity intensity.
howToUse: Read before choosing a metric and cut-point strategy.
why: Builds shared vocabulary across method and applied teams.
example: Onboarding reading for new Fellows joining a cohort study.

title: Open metrics for movement behaviour: ENMO, MAD and beyond
summary: Compares device-agnostic acceleration metrics and their trade-offs.
howToUse: Select a metric matched to your research question and device.
why: Open metrics enable cross-study comparison without proprietary units.
example: Informed metric harmonisation in a multi-cohort consortium.
limitations: Brand-specific counts are out of scope.

title: Window length choices in epoch-based analysis
summary: How epoch length shifts estimates of activity and sedentary time.
howToUse: Pre-register epoch length and report sensitivity.
why: Epoch choice silently changes headline results.
example: Used to justify a 5-second epoch in a gait study.

title: Movement phenotypes across the life course: a scoping review
summary: Surveys how behavioural phenotypes are defined and used across ages.
howToUse: Position your phenotyping approach against existing definitions.
example: Framed phenotype definitions for a youth activity study.
limitations: Coverage is lighter for clinical populations.

title: Compositional data analysis for the 24-hour activity cycle
summary: Why the day is a closed composition and how to model it correctly.
howToUse: Transform time-use data before modelling outcomes.
why: Ignoring the constant-sum constraint biases estimates.
example: Underpins isotemporal substitution in stage 6 tools.

title: Defining behavioural archetypes from wearable data
summary: Practical steps and pitfalls when labelling movement archetypes.
howToUse: Follow the validation steps before naming clusters.
example: Used to standardise archetype reporting in two studies.

title: Reallocating time between sleep, sedentary and activity: a causal lens
summary: Frames time-reallocation questions in causal terms with clear assumptions.
howToUse: State assumptions explicitly before substitution modelling.
why: Clarifies what isotemporal estimates can and cannot claim.
example: Guided interpretation of a sleep-for-activity swap analysis.
limitations: Causal claims rest on untestable assumptions.

title: Mendelian randomisation with device-measured physical activity
summary: Using genetic instruments to probe causality in activity–health links.
howToUse: Check instrument validity before applying MR.
why: Offers a complementary route around confounding.
example: Applied to activity and cardiometabolic outcomes.
limitations: Requires large genotyped samples and valid instruments.

title: Confounding in physical activity epidemiology
summary: Common confounders and reverse-causation traps, with mitigation tactics.
howToUse: Use the DAG templates to plan adjustment sets.
example: Informed covariate selection in a mortality study.

title: Visualising the 24-hour day: a design guide
summary: Design principles for honest, readable movement-behaviour graphics.
howToUse: Apply the checklist when designing figures.
why: Better visuals improve interpretation and uptake.
example: Used to redesign a cohort report dashboard.

title: Communicating uncertainty in movement behaviour estimates
summary: Ways to show confidence and variability without overwhelming readers.
howToUse: Pick an uncertainty encoding matched to the audience.
why: Hidden uncertainty leads to overconfident decisions.
example: Guided uncertainty bands in a public summary.

title: Translating accelerometer evidence into public-health guidance
summary: A pathway from device-measured evidence to actionable recommendations.
howToUse: Use the pathway to structure an evidence-to-guidance project.
why: Keeps recommendations traceable to underlying data.
example: Framed a national activity guideline update.
limitations: Process-focused; not a substitute for expert panels.

title: From data to policy: a framework for movement behaviour recommendations
summary: Connects analysis outputs to the needs of decision-makers.
howToUse: Align outputs to decision questions early.
example: Used to brief a municipal health team.
limitations: Assumes some analytic capacity in the audience.

title: Equity considerations in physical-activity policy
summary: How to surface and address equity when turning evidence into policy.
howToUse: Run the equity checklist alongside impact estimates.
why: Average effects can mask widening disparities.
example: Applied to a workplace activity programme review.
limitations: Qualitative; pairs best with stratified analysis.
